text: The surgical team docks the X-eye robotic system using a laser-guided crossbow to align the optics precisely for the procedure.
The X-eye robotic system is precisely docked using a laser-guided crossbow for optimal optical alignment.
After placing the ports, the surgeon begins dissection below the inferior mesenteric artery using monopolar diathermy, following a standardized approach.

The surgeon performs precise dissection at the posterior aspect of the superior rectal artery, carefully avoiding injury to the adjacent hypogastric nerves.

The surgeon isolates the inferior mesenteric artery at its origin, creating a window through careful dissection. The hypogastric nerve and left ureter are safeguarded during this process to facilitate the ligation and division of the artery.

Hemolog clips are applied to the inferior mesenteric artery to secure it, followed by division using scissors.
Hemolog clips are applied to the inferior mesenteric artery to secure it, followed by division of the artery using scissors.
During the abdominal perineal dissection, the surgeon takes the vein close to the artery, as planned, without the need to mobilize the left spinal flexure.
During the abdominal perineal dissection, the surgeon takes the vein close to the artery, as mobilization of the left splenic flexure is not required.
The surgeon divides the femoral ventricular vein, demonstrating the embryological plane in front of Gerota's fascia, highlighted by a thin white line, in a slim patient.

The surgeon performs precise dissection using paint-like movements with a monopolar diathermy hook or scissors, carefully navigating around the superior rectal artery to protect the hypogastric nerve.

The surgeon performs precise dissection using paint-like movements with a monopolar diathermy hook or scissors, facilitating the separation of tissue layers around the superior rectal artery while protecting the hypogastric nerve.
After mobilizing the left colon for stoma extraction, the surgeon begins pelvic dissection, focusing on the anatomical structures within the pelvic cavity.
After mobilizing the left colon for stoma extraction, the surgeon shifts focus to pelvic dissection, preparing the surgical field for further colorectal procedure steps.
The surgeon begins posterior dissection between the hypogastric nerves using an angulated robotic instrument, ensuring protection of both the pre-sacral fascia and the nerves.
The surgeon begins posterior dissection between the hypogastric nerves using an angulated robotic instrument, ensuring protection of both the pre-sacral fascia and the nerves.
The surgeon divides the peritoneum at the level of Douglas pouch following posterior dissection, carefully safeguarding the back wall of the vagina.
The surgeon divides the peritoneum at the level of the Douglas pouch, carefully safeguarding the posterior wall of the vagina during the procedure.
The surgeon performs precise posterior dissection using a monopolar diathermic scissor, applying gentle traction to separate tissue layers effectively.
The surgeon performs precise posterior dissection using monopolar diathermic scissors, applying gentle traction to separate tissue layers effectively.

The surgeon approaches the pelvic floor, focusing on mobilizing the specimen from the left pelvic side wall. Anterior dissection is performed close to the back wall of the vagina, where visible tethering of tissue is noted.

The surgeon carefully frees the left pelvic side wall, noting tethering of the vaginal wall despite preoperative imaging suggesting it was clear.

The surgeon carefully identifies the left ureter and the left hypogastric nerve, ensuring they are not inadvertently pulled or damaged during manipulation of the specimen.

The surgeon carefully identifies the left ureter and the left hypogastric nerve, visible in the surgical field. The angulated robotic instrument is used with precision to avoid excessive friction on the specimen, highlighted in the high-definition view.
The surgeon uses an angulated instrument to perform precise dissection, visible in high definition, focusing on the pelvic region during the abdominoperineal resection.
The surgeon carefully dissects the tethered back wall of the vagina, progressing safely to avoid proximity to the tumor. The left pelvic side wall is visible, revealing the pelvic floor.

The surgeon continues dissection anteriorly, with the back wall of the vagina visible in the surgical field. A small piece of the vaginal backbone is removed and included with the specimen to ensure an R-zero resection, avoiding positive margins.

The surgeon continues dissection anteriorly, revealing the back wall of the vagina. A small piece of the vaginal backbone is removed and included with the specimen to ensure an R-zero dissection, avoiding a positive margin.
The surgeon continues dissection on both the right and left sides, revealing the endopelvic fascia and pelvic floor clearly within the surgical field.

The surgeon performs a gentle digital examination on the back wall of the vagina, facilitating the safe dissection of the outer lining layers. A small disc of vaginal tissue is left intact from the specimen.

The surgeon continues the dissection past the previously tethered area, revealing a clear, soft tissue plane. The dissection progresses further in a completely bloodless field.

The surgeon continues the dissection past the previously tethered back wall of the vagina, revealing a clear, soft tissue plane. The dissection progresses further in a bloodless manner, maintaining precision and avoiding damage to surrounding structures.
A swab is inserted into a small hole in the vaginal wall to minimize gas leakage and maintain pneumoperitoneum during the procedure.

The camera is positioned at a thirty-degree upward angle, providing a static view of the pelvic floor muscles from a posterior perspective.

The surgeon performs a modified abdominal perineal resection from above, carefully excising minimal pelvic floor muscle to achieve an R-zero dissection, due to the luminal regrowth of the patient's tumor.
The surgeon performs a modified abdominal perineal resection from above, sacrificing minimal pelvic floor muscle due to the luminal regrowth, aiming for an R-zero dissection.

The surgeon uses an angulated instrument to perform precise dissection in the pelvic region, with a stable camera view showing the progress of the surgery as it reaches lower anatomical levels in the patient.
The surgical team uses a stable camera view and angulated instruments to perform precise dissection, progressing safely and reaching as low as possible in the pelvic region.
The surgeon enters the intersphincteric plane posteriorly, continuing the dissection in a clear, bloodless plane.
The surgeon enters the intersphincteric plane posteriorly, revealing a clear soft tissue plane with minimal bleeding.
The surgeon performs a digital examination both inside the lumen and externally on the skin, confirming the anatomical structures and surgical progress.
The surgeon performs a digital examination both inside the lumen and externally on the skin, confirming the anatomical structures and surgical progress.

The surgeon completes the posterior dissection by mobilizing the specimen and using electrocautery to burn through the tissue onto the skin extraluminally, ensuring the dissection reaches the skin surface from above.
The surgeon completes the posterior dissection by mobilizing the specimen and using electrocautery to burn through onto the skin extraluminally, achieving full exposure from above.

The surgeon performs anterior dissection in the left pelvic side bowl, revealing the intersphincteric plane. Gentle traction and counter traction are applied to achieve precise anatomical views during this step.

The surgeon performs anterior dissection in the left pelvic side bowl, revealing the intersphincteric plane. Gentle traction and counter traction are applied to maintain precision and provide clear anatomical views during the procedure.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to perform circumferential dissection on both the left and right sides, accessing the intersphincteric groove and reaching the skin edge. After complete mobilization of the specimen, a small defect in the vagina is closed with two interrupted POPDS stitches.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to perform a circumferential dissection on both the left and right sides, accessing the intersphincteric groove and reaching the skin edge. After fully mobilizing the specimen, a small defect in the vagina is closed with two interrupted POPDS stitches.
The surgeon mobilizes a small cuff of skin posteriorly from below to facilitate the evacuation of the specimen.
The surgeon mobilizes a small cuff of skin posteriorly from below to facilitate the evacuation of the specimen.
The surgeon divides the blood supply of the left colon proximally, preparing to fashion a left ventricular cyclostomy.
The surgeon divides the blood supply of the left colon proximally to create a left ventricular cyclostomy.
The surgeon uses a bipolar device to extract the specimen from the perineum. The excised specimen is visually confirmed to be an adenocarcinoma with clear margins.
The surgeon uses a bipolar device to extract the specimen from the perineum. The excised specimen is shown, revealing a completely excised adenocarcinoma with clear margins.
